attributes.stem:
A three-month-old boy presents to the Emergency Department with fever and cellulitis of his right leg. His parent reports that his umbilical cord did not fall off until eight weeks of age, and he has had three previous admissions for skin infections. He has no other medical history and takes no regular medications. His immunisations are up to date.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 160/min, blood pressure 85/50 mmHg, respiratory rate 45/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 39.2°C. There is a 5 cm area of erythema, warmth and induration on his right thigh. His umbilical stump is well-healed.

Blood tests reveal: White cell count 85 × 10⁹/L (4-11), with neutrophils 75 × 10⁹/L (2-7).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This infant presents with features highly suggestive of leukocyte adhesion deficiency (LAD). The key diagnostic features are delayed umbilical cord separation (beyond 3-4 weeks), recurrent bacterial infections, and marked neutrophilia during infection. In leukocyte adhesion deficiency, neutrophils cannot adhere to blood vessel walls and migrate to sites of infection due to defective CD18 (beta-2 integrin) expression.

Chronic granulomatous disease is characterised by recurrent infections with catalase-positive organisms and formation of granulomas, but does not feature delayed cord separation or marked neutrophilia.

Hyper-IgE syndrome (Job syndrome) presents with recurrent infections but is typically associated with eczema, characteristic facial features, and elevated IgE levels, which are not present here.

Severe combined immunodeficiency typically presents with failure to thrive, persistent infections, and opportunistic organisms. These patients have lymphopenia rather than neutrophilia.

Kostmann syndrome (severe congenital neutropenia) is characterised by severe neutropenia from birth, which is the opposite of what we see in this case.

The diagnosis of LAD can be confirmed by flow cytometry showing absent or reduced CD18 expression on leukocytes. Genetic testing for mutations in the ITGB2 gene, which encodes CD18, can provide definitive diagnosis.
learningPoints: Leukocyte adhesion deficiency typically presents in infancy with delayed umbilical cord separation and recurrent bacterial infections.
Leukocyte adhesion deficiency is characterised by markedly elevated neutrophil counts during infections due to inability of neutrophils to migrate to sites of inflammation.
The combination of delayed umbilical cord separation, recurrent bacterial infections, and marked neutrophilia is highly suggestive of leukocyte adhesion deficiency.
references: Almarza Novoa E, Kasbekar S, Thrasher AJ, et al. Leukocyte adhesion deficiency-I: A comprehensive review of all published cases. J Allergy Clin Immunol Pract. 2018;6(4):1418-1420.e10. doi:10.1016/j.jaip.2017.12.008
